title: Financial Market Ethics
abstract: We develop a model of psychological-games-played-on-a-network to demonstrate a role for endogenously determined, rationally chosen ethics. Our analysis produces sharp results about contagion of nonethical or ethical behavior and the possible equilibrium configurations of each type of behavior. We find, and quantify, critical densities for clusters of each type of behavior that determine everything about contagion. We introduce society as a third player to investigate ethical failures as externalities. We use these results to show how regulations and network structure can affect whether clusters of ethical behavior can survive and how large they can be in a financial market setting.

author.Maureen O‚ÄôHara*: Johnson College of Business, Cornell University, USA

article.1. Related Literature:
JEL A13 - Relation of Economics to Social Values C70 - General G28 - Government Policy and Regulation G41 - Role and Effects of Psychological, Emotional, Social, and Cognitive Factors on Decision Making in Financial Markets Issue Section: Article Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Ethics is conspicuously absent in standard economic models. This failure to show up is not inadvertent; as Nobel laureate Kenneth Arrow explained, ‚ÄúLike many economists, I do not want to rely too heavily on substituting ethics for self-interest. I think it best on the whole that the requirement of ethical behavior be confined to those circumstances where the price system breaks down.‚ÄĚ1This view of ethics as a last resort, as an overlay needed only when the system is broken, draws on a traditional belief in economics that markets work best when rational agents all act in their own self-interest. Yet, in actual markets, ethics (or the lack thereof) seem to play a large role in affecting not only the performance of a market, but also its viability. Moreover, the financial crisis and its aftermath suggest that nonethical behavior can spread, with such behavior in one market inducing similar malfeasance in others. Viewing economic agents simply as autonomous members of ‚ÄúHomo Economicus‚ÄĚ seems inadequate to capture these market effects.

In this paper we investigate the role of market ethics. We are interested in two basic issues. First, can rational agents care about ethical concerns, and can those agents survive in equilibrium? And second, can ethical (or nonethical) behavior propagate in a market? Are there properties of agent networks in which nonethical behavior spreads from node to node, and if so, how can it be stopped? Conversely, under what conditions can ethical behavior be contagious?

To address these issues, we draw on psychological game theory and the theory of contagion in networks. We use a game-theoretic model that allows rational agents to care about others‚Äô expectations of their behavior. Agents in our model are not inherently ethical or nonethical but instead exhibit a type of ‚Äúrational morality.‚ÄĚ We characterize agents‚Äô equilibrium strategies in a game in which they choose between ethical and nonethical behavior, with a focus on when agents switch from playing ethically to playing nonethically. We then embed this game in a network setting and examine how the structure of the network and the interaction of heterogeneous agents result in cascades of ethical or nonethical behavior. Our analysis of psychological games played on a network is, we believe, an important technical contribution of our paper.

Our focus is on the financial markets where networks characterize many types of interactions, and issues relating to ethics and integrity have drawn renewed interest following the global financial crisis. Particularly relevant is the Bank of England‚Äďsponsored study that identified ‚Äúa process of `ethical drift‚Äô where unethical behavior went unchecked and hence became progressively more widespread and accepted as the norm.‚ÄĚ2The report attributed this rise of financial market misconduct to failures in both the ‚Äúhard infrastructure‚ÄĚ (i.e., regulation) and the ‚Äúsoft infrastructure,‚ÄĚ defined by standards and norms. This latter ‚Äúsoft‚ÄĚ effect is what concerns us here‚ÄĒwe seek to capture these ethical dimensions in a cogent economic model that illuminates not only why ethics matters, but also how to move markets to more ethical equilibria.

To motivate our analysis, consider the recent case of ‚Äúmanufactured defaults‚ÄĚ in the credit default swap (CDS) market.3Here Blackstone bought CDSs on a firm and simultaneously lent money to that firm with the precondition that the firm delay its next bond payment‚ÄĒthereby guaranteeing a hefty payoff for Blackstone when the delay triggered swap payments. While not illegal, this behavior was widely viewed as unethical, breaking as it did the accepted norms of behavior in the market, and eliciting a rare joint statement from the SEC, CFTC, and the U.K. Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) decrying the impact of ‚Äúopportunistic strategies in credit derivative markets‚ÄĚ on market integrity.4Indeed, the general market condemnation forced Blackstone to back down on enforcing its claims, suggesting an ethical equilibrium prevailed. But while quelled in this setting, the strategy spread. Aurelius Capital applied a similar CDS-based strategy to force Wirestream Holdings into bankruptcy,5and similar issues have arisen in the U.S. leveraged loan market.6Taking this strategy even further afield, Brookfield launched a takeover bid for Inter Pipeline Inc. in February 2021, using total return swaps (TRS) to gain economic exposure while avoiding the Canadian threshold for reporting requirements in takeovers.7Are these examples of contagion of nonethical behavior prevailing in equilibrium? In this paper we provide a framework for understanding when such disparate outcomes can arise and what can be done to move market equilibria from ‚Äúbad‚ÄĚ outcomes to ‚Äúgood‚ÄĚ ones.

What distinguishes our analysis from more standard models is our use of psychological games. Psychological game theory, which was developed byGeanakoplos, Pearce, and Stacchetti (1989), extended byBattigalli and Dufwenberg (2009)and applied to guilt in games byBattigalli and Dufwenberg (2007), allows payoffs to depend directly on beliefs rather than just on actions, as in traditional game theory. In our application of psychological game theory, a player experiences guilt, and thus a reduction in payoff, if he disappoints others. This disappointment aversion, or guilt, is modeled as a reduction in payoff based on the player‚Äôs expectation of others‚Äô expectation of his behavior.8The key point in our analysis of market ethics is that this payoff reduction from behaving nonethically is endogenous; it occurs only when others expect ethical behavior. A player who is expected to behave nonethically experiences no guilt from nonethical behavior as he disappoints no one with this behavior.9

This notion of caring about what others expect us to do is reminiscent of Adam Smith‚Äôs theory of moral sentiments. Smith argued that happiness is firmly rooted in the approbation of others, and this required individuals to act so as to satisfy the expectations of an ‚Äúimpartial spectator.‚ÄĚ Failure to do so would bring on fear of discovery, pangs of conscience, and other unease. Smith envisioned each of us as incorporating such an internal impartial spectator, so that our choices reflect not only our immediate wants but also our moral sentiments connected with the expectations of others.10

Using a psychological games framework, we begin with a simple two-player game in which guilt induces two pure strategy equilibria (ethical and nonethical). What matters for our purposes here is what happens to these equilibria in a setting with multiple agents, which we model as a network. We show how the network structure and the parameters in the game affect the evolution of ethical and nonethical behavior.

Our analysis demonstrates that contagion of behavior is determined by two separate characteristics. First, the attractiveness of ethical behavior relative to the attractiveness of nonethical behavior matters, as it affects how easy or difficult it is for nonethical behavior to spread from one agent to another. Second, the local structure of the network matters via the density of clusters that describe whether any group of agents is tightly connected or has members who are exposed to many agents who are not part of the group. These two factors interact to determine whether nonethical behavior spreads throughout the network or whether ethical behavior persists.11

Our analysis builds on the contagion-in-networks results ofMorris (2000). Morris showed that if agents in a network play a simple coordination game with their neighbors, then contagion of behavior is controlled by the density of clusters of agents. We, too, find that clusters are determinant, but the critical density of clusters is more complex in our analysis as the game that agents play is more complex; it is a psychological game. This matters, as depending on the details of the game we find critical cluster densities that are expectation dependent, and in some cases, we find multiple critical densities allowing small clusters of disparate behaviors to coexist.

We show that if ethical behavior persists, it occurs only in dense clusters of agents, all of whom behave ethically. We also demonstrate how parameter values, particularly the guilt parameter, determine how difficult it is to maintain ethical behavior in the network. In an extension of the model, we allow some agents to be ‚Äúhard-wired‚ÄĚ for ethical behavior and show how depending on their placement in the network such nodes can have a global effect on dampening the spread of nonethical behavior in the network, even if there are few ethical nodes. We also characterize how specific network structures often found in financial markets abet or retard the spread of nonethical behavior in a network.

Finally, we consider a modification of our two-player game to include society as a third player. In this modification, players have beliefs about what society expects of them, and experience guilt if they behave nonethically when society expects them to play ethically. A natural interpretation of this three-player game is that the interaction between our two active players can impose a negative externality on society when the players behave nonethically. For example, their nonethical behavior may reduce society‚Äôs confidence in the workings of the market. In this setting, society plays the role of Smith‚Äôs impartial spectator. We show that our analysis of the stability of clusters and contagion of behavior applies directly to this modified game.

Given these results, what can be done to influence outcomes so that ethical rather than nonethical behavior prevails? We demonstrate how regulation might be structured to accomplish this, both by changing payoffs in the game and by targeting particular nodes in the network. This redesigned role for regulation follows from two intriguing results: the importance of a critical value for the density of a cluster of ethical behavior and the identity of critical nodes. There are other factors that also influence equilibrium outcomes. We discuss how different market structures may be more or less amenable to the proliferation of nonethical behavior, and how this might be overcome. We also show how society‚Äôs (and regulators‚Äô) expectations play a role by enforcing the impact of others‚Äô expectations on individuals‚Äô choices. Overall, our research suggests that market ethics need not be an oxymoron, but it can be a fragile outcome; understanding why this fragility emerges is crucial for ensuring that ‚Äúbetter‚ÄĚ outcomes prevail.

While our interest in this paper is on understanding ethical outcomes in financial markets, we believe the psychological games played on a network approach have applicability to a wide variety of other settings. As we discuss in the conclusion, questions such as why criminality spreads, or why graft is so hard to remove in some economies, or why some corporate cultures succeed while others fail, may all be better understood by recognizing how others‚Äô expectations of others‚Äô beliefs affect outcomes in network settings. Perhaps more important is the potential that we demonstrate for a player‚Äôs nonethical behavior to spread through the network and perhaps make other players worse off. Contagious nonethical behavior is an externality‚ÄĒit is harmful to others, even those far away from the initial nonethical player, and there is no compensation for its effects on others. Compensation through the price system is hard to imagine, as although nonethical behavior may be observable to a player‚Äôs neighbors, it may not be externally verifiable. Even it could be verified, the notion of pricing nonethical behavior might in itself destroy the guilt that helps limit its spread. Why feel guilty about an action that society has legitimized by pricing it? Our interest is in understanding this externality and asking what society can do to limit its harmful effects.
Although our focus on ethics in the context of psychological games played in a network is new, there is a variety of work that considers related issues.Glazer, Sacerdote, and Scheinkman (1996)developed the idea that culture plays a role in a decision to respect rules or break them, and there is a large body of work looking at the impact of culture and its determinants (see, for example,Cremer 1993;Guiso, Sapienza, and Zingales 2006;Grullon, Kanatas, and Weston 2009;Liu 2016;Lo 2016;Mokyr 2016). An important difference between our work and these research areas is that in this research, culture and social norms are considered exogenous, while ethics in our framework arises endogenously from beliefs about others‚Äô beliefs, allowing us to characterize how such behaviors can propagate in a population. More closely related to our research is work on social norms, which relates behavior to various norms governing participants‚Äô action and often derives the stability of these norms from an evolutionary model (see, for example,Elster 1989;Ostrom 2000; andYoung 2015on social norms and their evolution). Also closely related to our work is the study byAlger and Weibull (2013), which takes an evolutionary approach to select among preferences for one‚Äôs own payoffs and others‚Äô payoffs. They show that a random matching model in which selected pairs play a two-person game selects for preferences that cause players to act as if they care about morality.

Our analysis of ethics shares motivation with this literature, but our focus on psychological games played on networks as the origin of ethical behavior leads to different insights into ethical behavior. There are, however, several papers that either use psychological games or have socially motivated payoffs.Huck, Kubler, and Weibull (2012)consider a model in which individuals in a team choose efforts and receive material utility from these effort levels and social utility depending on their own effort in relation to others‚Äô efforts and their socially ideal effort. They examine the interaction of compensation contracts and social norms arising from this psychological approach. More closely related is the literature examining reciprocity in games played by a network of agents (seeDufwenberger and Patel 2017for a theoretical analysis orLeider et al. 2009for an experimental analysis). This research makes use of psychological game theory, but the focus is different from ours in that we are interested in the evolution of ethical or nonethical behavior on the network, while they primarily focus on how friendship structures as represented by a network affect apparent altruism.

Our work is also related to the large literature on cascading behavior in networks. In this literature, individuals imitate or seem to imitate the behavior of their neighbors: interactions take place at the local level; an individual‚Äôs payoffs depend on what they do and on what their neighbors do and not on the actions of others who may be far away in the network. In this literature, an individual may find it payoff-enhancing to copy the behavior of neighbors either because of the information revealed by the play of neighbors (seeBanerjee 1992orBikhchandani, Hirshleifer, and Welch 1992for the classic papers) or because there is a direct payoff benefit to behaving like its neighbors (seeKatz and Shapiro 1985orArthur 1990,1994for the early literature on direct benefit effects without a network andMorris 2000for a network model that we build from). This can lead to contagion of behavior or herding. In our work it is payoff effects that matter, but these payoff effects are subtle, as they arise from the potential for guilt created by playing nonethically when neighbors expect ethical play. We show that if a large enough fraction of a node‚Äôs neighbors behave ethically (or nonethically), then it is payoff-enhancing for the node to also behave ethically (or nonethically). This can lead to a cascade of behavior in which individuals seem to copy the behavior of their neighbors, although in our setting they do so because behaving like your neighbors has a direct benefit.

Another large, recent body of work examines contagion in financial networks (seeBenoit et al. 2017andGlasserman and Young 2016for recent surveys). Much of this literature analyzes the role of the network between financial firms in creating systemic risk. Most papers consider the effect of more or less densely connected, but exogenously given networks (see, for example,Allen and Gale 2000;Eisenberg and Noe 2001;Freixas, Parigi, and Rochet 2000; Allen and Babus 2008;Gai, Haldane, and Kapadia 2011;Acemoglu et al. 2015;Elliott, Golub, and Jackson 2014), while a few (Blume et al. 2013;Erol and Vohra 2014;Babus 2016) consider endogenously determined networks. Our analysis of the spread of nonethical behavior is also about contagion on a financial network; however, the underlying interactions between agents are quite different in our setting. Our agents are playing a psychological game on the exogenous network, and we are interested in contagion of behavior, rather than contagion of defaults leading to systemic risk.

Our work is also related toYoung (1996), and the literature on conventions discussed there, as well as toMorris (2000)andJackson and Storms (2019). This literature, discussing multiple conventions in population games, stability of clusters of behavior on a network, and communities of agents in networks who always behave the same way, studies groups of agents who play the same way or who adopt the same behavior. We, too, ask about groups playing similarly, but our use of psychological games allows us to describe and analyze the evolution of ethical or nonethical play.

An aspect of our work not addressed in any of these related research areas is our focus on an important policy question: How can market design and regulation facilitate ethical behavior? This question is of obvious importance, and there is a growing body of work empirically investigating financial misconduct. Research here focuses on behaviors such as option back-dating (Lie 2005;Bernile and Jarrell 2009), earnings management (Kedia, Koh, and Rajgopal 2015), SEC violations (Karpoff et al. 2017;Parsons, Sulaeman, and Titman 2018), and financial advisor fraud (Dimmock, Gerken, and Graham 2018;Liu 2016;Egan, Matvos, and Seru 2019).12Our theoretical work complements and provides an explanation for some of the important findings here. For example,Parsons, Sulaeman, and Titman (2018)find empirically that financial misconduct occurs in clusters (by cities) and that there are ebbs and flows in the propensity to commit misconduct over time. Our results offer one explanation for why such clusters emerge and how network dynamics determine their movement over time. Similarly, a variety of papers find that financial misconduct appears to be contagious.13For example,Dimmock, Gerken, and Graham (2018)find evidence of contagion effects, showing that fraud by financial advisors is more likely if new coworkers have a history of fraud.Liu (2016)finds a similar effect when insiders move from companies with a low corruption index to companies with a high corruption index.Egan, Matvos, and Seru (2019)show that one-third of advisors with misconduct are repeat offenders and that firms that hire such advisors have higher rates of misconduct. Such effects are consistent both with the psychological games framework developed here and with the predictions of our model with respect to the propagation of nonethical behavior in networks.
article.2. Games, Networks, and Contagion:
In this section, we set out the psychological game in which players choose to play ethically or nonethically. A useful first step is to discuss what we mean by ethical and nonethical behavior. In our setting, ethical behavior arises when agents care about others‚Äô expectations of their behavior. These ethical considerations can result in agents choosing actions that lead to lower monetary rewards so as to avoid disappointing others. Such considerations do not arise from expectations of nonethical play; if you are expected to play nonethically and you choose to play ethically, you do not feel guilt.14There are, of course, other permutations of how guilt attaches that could be considered (we consider one alternative in Section4.3), but this specification lucidly captures the essence of the problem. Note that we use the terminology nonethical instead of unethical. It seems likely that many nonethical behaviors are also unethical, but we use the broader terminology to capture a potentially broader range of behaviors.

2.1. The game

Our analysis of the diffusion of ethical or nonethical behavior through a network is built on a basic model of the interaction between agents in two-player games. These agents are embedded in an underlying network in which they each have a choice in each period between two types of behavior: ethical (‚Ā†E‚Ā†) and nonethical (‚Ā†N‚Ā†). If there is an edge between two agents, they play the game with each other, and they each receive payoffs from their joint play.15We assume that each agent must choose one strategy to use in all of its interactions.16

Although agents will play this game many times, we assume that they do not view their interaction as part of a repeated game. Instead, in each period they play a one-period game. In theOnline Appendixwe analyze the infinitely repeated game and show that for sufficiently low, but nonzero discount factors, the play that we describe in the text is consistent with a subgame perfect psychological Nash equilibrium of the infinite horizon game.17In this equilibrium, agents do not build and exploit reputations. Instead, they act as if they were playing the one-period game repeatedly with each game played in isolation.

Before beginning the formal analysis of this game, it may be useful to contrast it with standard games and explain why we use this particular game. In standard game theory, payoffs depend only on actions‚ÄĒnot on beliefs. Of course, which action a player chooses may depend on beliefs, but once actions are selected, payoffs are determined independently of beliefs. In the psychological game we consider, that separation between beliefs and payoffs does not hold. In our game, payoffs depend both on what the players do and on what they believe about each other. Our players experience guilt, and thus a payoff reduction, from behaving nonethically if they believe that the other player expected them to play ethically. This endogeneity of guilt, and its effect on the dynamics of play in the network, is crucial for our analysis and for our interpretation of results. It makes play, and the eventual stable configuration of play, history dependent, sensitive to the structure of the network, and amenable to changes in regulations.18

2.2. Network

We view financial markets as networks. Some, such as interbank networks and networks of dealers, are explicitly networks. Others, such as exchanges and trading rooms, are less explicitly networks. But they can all be described by a collection of nodes (the banks, dealers, or traders) connected by edges representing who-interacts-with-whom.

The critical value condition can be interpreted using the notion of strategic complementarity. Strategic complementarity applied to our game requires that the difference in the payoff to playingErelative to the payoff to playingNis increasing in the fraction of neighbors playingE‚Ā†. This follows from our base model assumption. So as the fraction of its neighbors playingEincreases, a node finds playingErather thanNmore and more attractive. Similarly, forp<p‚ąó‚Ā†, playingEis less attractive, and the node playsN‚Ā†.

Our model induces a threshold rule, as inMorris (2000). A node‚Äôs play is determined by the fraction of its neighbors playing ethically; the number of neighbors does not matter, and payoffs affect play only through their effect onp*. However, ours is not a simple threshold, asp‚ąódepends both on the parameters of the game and on expectations of play. Multiple correct expectations are possible in the underlying game, which naturally raises the question of where players‚Äô expectations come from. In our network model, the interaction between players is local; each player needs only local knowledge of the network and his neighbors‚Äô play. Given his local information, in each period, no player has a reason to expect anyone to change their play from last period, as it was a best response to the player‚Äôs own play last period.23Of course, his neighbors may change their behavior either through an exogenous shock or in response to changes in the behavior of others, and if they do, the player may change his behavior in subsequent periods.

In our standard use of the threshold rule (seeMorris 2000for the development of the theory orEasley and Kleinberg 2010for an exposition of it), players believe that the fraction of their neighbors who currently play a strategy will be the fraction who played it last period. Their expectations are correct in a steady state, but may be wrong while play is evolving in the network.24

We consider in Section4.1the case in whicha>cand a player may switch from nonethical play to ethical play if enough of his neighbors play ethically.
article.3. The Dynamics of Play with Dominant No:
The analytical question we begin with is: Starting from a network in which everyone is ethical or everyone is nonethical, can a small number of nodes exogenously flipping to the other type of behavior cause that new behavior to spread through the network possibly flipping everyone to the new behavior?25This group of initial switchers may simply be trying out a new behavior to see what happens. This new behavior is not a best response to what others are currently doing, so it is initially payoff-reducing. But perhaps they are interested in observing how others respond to their new behavior. Alternatively, they could simply be trembling‚ÄĒmistakenly choosing a new strategy. In either case, we ask whether this new behavior diffuses throughout the network or whether it stops before becoming widespread.

Obviously, widespread diffusion of ethical play cannot happen if every node begins with nonethical play, as given past nonethical play, playing nonethically is dominant. So, a network of nonethical play is stable. Alternatively, if every node initially plays ethically, and a small number of nodes flip to nonethical play, the eventual outcome depends on the parameters of the game and the structure of the network. Most importantly, whether these flipped nodes cause other nodes to flip depends on what fraction of their neighbors are playing nonethically.

The dynamic process induced by these flipped nodes evolves as follows:

Initially, each node is labeled with the same play: allEor allN‚Ā†.Next, the labels for some set of nodes are exogenously switched fromEtoNif all were initially labeledE‚Ā†, or fromNtoEif all were initially labeledN‚Ā†.Then, each node expecting its neighbors to play as labeled and knowing that its neighbors expect it to play as labeled selects a best response.The nodes are then labeled with these best responses and step (3) is repeated.The process is declared to have stopped if in two successive labelings, no labels are modified.

To state the results for the outcome of this dynamic process, we first need a few definitions. A cluster of densitypis a set of nodes such that each node in the set has at least a fractionpof its neighbors in the set.26A dense cluster is a set of nodes for which most of their interaction with other nodes occurs within the set rather than with nodes outside of the set. Of course, the entire network is a cluster of density one.

Consider a network in which every node initially playsE‚Ā†. We say that a set of nodes,S‚Ā†, changing their behavior toNcauses a complete cascade if when the nodes inSchange toNthen eventually every node in the networks playsN‚Ā†.Morris (2000)shows that, for the structure he analyzes, whether complete cascades occur or not is determined entirely by whether sufficiently dense clusters exist or not. His analysis, modified to accommodate our psychological game, applies to our setting.

Theorem 1 Suppose the parameters of the game satisfy the basic model assumption and c > a ‚Ā† . Consider a network in which every node initially plays E ‚Ā† . Suppose that a set S of nodes switches to behavior N ‚Ā† . (i) If the remaining network, consisting of nodes in J ‚ąí S and the edges between them, contains a cluster of density at least p ‚ąó ‚ąó ‚Ā† , then a complete cascade does not occur. (ii) If a complete cascade does not occur, then the remaining network must contain a cluster of density at least p ‚ąó ‚ąó ‚Ā† .

All proofs are provided in Appendix A. This theorem is a modification of the argument inEasley and Kleinberg (2010)to our setting. Essentially this result says that clusters stop contagion of nonethical play and contagion can only be stopped by clusters.

The intuition for part (i) of Theorem 1 is straightforward. Suppose that the contagion ofNcould invade a cluster of nodes all playingEof density at leastp‚ąó‚ąó‚Ā†. Then there must be some first node in this cluster that flips toN‚Ā†, but at the time of the flip, at least the fractionp‚ąó‚ąóof its neighbors are playingE‚Ā†. Therefore, this node will not flip, and thus no node in the cluster flips. The intuition for part (ii) is similar. If a complete cascade does not occur, then some nodes remain playingE‚Ā†. Unless those nodes are in a cluster of nodes, all playingE‚Ā†, of density at leastp‚ąó‚ąó‚Ā†, then at least one of them wants to switch toN‚Ā†. But then the cascade has not stopped.

We now turn to our second analytical question: Does the process stop, and if it does stop, what limit structure of ethical and nonethical play is possible? We imagine a network in which exogenous shocks are rare and first ask if the dynamic process stops. In our framework any node that exogenously switches toNcontinues to playN‚Ā†, and any node that is induced to playNbecause of the play of its neighbors will not switch back toE‚Ā†. There are a finite number of nodes, so the process stops in finite time either with all nodes playingNor some playingNand the remaining nodes playingE‚Ā†. This guarantees that following an exogenous shock with some nodes switching their behavior, the process of behavioral adjustment converges in finite time.

We say that a configuration of play,{ai}i‚ąąJwhere eachai‚ąą{E,N}‚Ā†, is a network equilibrium if: (i) each player is playing a best response in the network game, and (ii) all players‚Äô beliefs (both first and second order) about the play of their neighbors are correct.28Clearly, every node playingNis an equilibrium configuration of play. For a configuration of play in which some nodes playEto be an equilibrium, each of those nodes must have a fraction of at leastp‚ąó‚ąóof their neighbors playingE‚Ā†, as otherwise they would switch to playingN‚Ā†. Therefore, the dynamic process cannot stop unless the set of nodes playingEis a cluster with density at leastp‚ąó‚ąó‚Ā†. In any other configuration of play, some node wants to change its behavior.

Corollary 1 The dynamic process stops in finite time, and once it is stopped, the configuration of play is a network equilibrium.

Corollary 2 The equilibrium configurations of play are: All nodes play N ‚Ā† . Any configuration of nodes in which the cluster of all nodes playing E has density at least p ‚ąó ‚ąó (all other nodes play N ‚Ā† ).

There are multiple equilibrium configurations of play, but in each of those equilibrium configurations, if any node playsE‚Ā†, then it is in a cluster of nodes playingEof density at leastp‚ąó‚ąó‚Ā†.29It is also useful to note that some equilibria in some networks are robust to a larger collection of shocks (sets of nodes flipping) than others. For example, in the two-node network, both nodes playingNis an equilibrium, and if one node flips toE‚Ā†, the other node continues to playN‚Ā†, and the flipped node changes back toN‚Ā†. In the two-node network, both nodes playingEis also an equilibrium, but if one node flips toN‚Ā†, then the process converges to all nodes playingN‚Ā†. So, in addition to limiting our attention to equilibria, it is useful to consider robustness to alternative sets of nodes flipping their behavior.

3.1. Examples of stable equilibria

Outcomes from the dynamic process depend on the structure of the network and on the details of the game being played. In this section we examine a few classic network structures and the possible configurations of equilibrium play. The networks we examine range from the simple circle selected for its analytical convenience to core-periphery networks and networks of nearly disjoint cliques analyzed because of their relevance for financial markets.

3.1.1. Circle network

A simple (finite) network in which all nodes are in a symmetric position and there is a path between any two nodes is a circle or ring. In this network each node has two neighbors, so1/2will be a critical value forp‚ąó‚ąó‚Ā†. Ifp‚ąó‚ąó>1/2‚Ā†, then the only equilibrium configurations of play are all nodes playingNor all playingE‚Ā†. To see this, note that as each node has two neighbors, if more than1/2of them playE‚Ā†, then they all playE‚Ā†. So, a cluster of density at leastp‚ąó‚ąóplayingEmust be a cluster of density 1. The only such cluster for a circle is the entire circle. Thus, either all nodes play ethically or they all play nonethically.

However, the configuration in which all nodes playEis fragile. Any perturbation in which one or more nodes switch toNcauses at least one nonperturbed node to have at most1/2of its neighbors playingE‚Ā†, and so that node switches toN‚Ā†. This process cannot stop until all nodes playN‚Ā†, as otherwise there is some node playingEwith at least one neighbor playingN‚Ā†. Thus, forp‚ąó‚ąó>1/2‚Ā†, the only robust equilibrium configuration in the circle is for all nodes to playN‚Ā†.

Alternatively, ifp‚ąó‚ąó‚Č§1/2‚Ā†, and one of its neighbors switches toN‚Ā†, any node whose other neighbor playsEwill continue to playE‚Ā†. So, equilibria with some nodes playingNand some playingEare possible. In this case, equilibrium configurations can have multiple clusters of nodes playingE‚Ā†, but all such clusters have density at leastp‚ąó‚ąó>0‚Ā†, and so must be of size at least 2.

3.1.2. Star network

The next level up in complexity that we analyze is the star. SeeBabus and Hu (2017)for a network formation model in the over-the-counter market in which a star arises endogenously. We suppose that the nodes are arranged as a star with one node, node 1, in the center and the otherI‚ąí1nodes are each linked to the center and have no other neighbors. If the center playsN‚Ā†, then clearly all nodes must playNin any network equilibrium. Any equilibrium configuration in which the center playsErequires that a set of peripheral nodes of sizeK‚Ā†, withK/(I‚ąí1)‚Č•p‚ąó‚ąó‚Ā†, also playE‚Ā†. This configuration is an equilibrium as the center, and the peripheral nodes playingEform a cluster of density at leastp‚ąó‚ąó‚Ā†. Configurations in which the center playsEand at leastK+1other nodes also playEare robust to any single peripheral node flipping toN‚Ā†, although they are clearly not robust to the center flipping toN‚Ā†. Therefore, ethical play in the star is not as fragile as it is in the circle, but it is not robust to perturbed behavior at the center.

3.1.3. Core-periphery network

The star is a special case of a core-periphery structure in which the core is of size one. There is a large literature showing that various financial networks exhibit a core-periphery structure: see, for example,Li and Schurhoff (2019)on municipal bond trading;Hollifield, Neklyudov, and Spatt (2017)on securitization markets; andBech and Atalay (2010)on the federal funds market. LetMbe the maximum number of periphery neighbors for any core node. All nodes playingEand all nodes playingNare network equilibria. 3.1.4. Cliques

Finally, we consider a network consisting of a collection of cliques with sparse edges between cliques. A clique is a subset of nodes such that every two distinct nodes in the clique are connected. Each clique in this type of network is generally resistant to contagion arising from outside of the clique, but a node within a clique that flips its behavior can create a cascade within the clique. This type of structure has been shown to arise both from individual link formation and from socially optimal link formation (seeBlume et al. 2013). Equilibria in this network can, depending on the density of the cliques and number of edges between them, consist of some cliques playingEand other cliques playingN‚Ā†.Figure 5illustrates a typical equilibrium configuration. In this figure, the nonethical play in the clique on the right cannot invade the ethical play in the other two cliques, as each clique is a cluster of ethical play that is sufficiently dense.

Figure 5 An equilibrium outcome with a collection of cliques Each clique has density 3 / 4 ‚Ā† . The red nodes all play ethically, and the green ones all play nonethically. This is an equilibrium configuration if p ‚ąó ‚ąó < 3 / 4 ‚Ā† . Open in new tab Download slide

This observation provides one explanation for the geographic clustering of ethical and nonethical behavior found inParsons, Sulaeman, and Titman (2018). They find that financial misconduct occurs in geographic clusters, and these clusters cannot be explained by geographic variation in firm characteristics or enforcement. Their explanation is geographic variation in social norms. If edges exist between all firms in the same geographic location (they form a clique) and there are fewer edges connecting firms across locations, then our network can be viewed as a geographical network in which clusters arise in cities. With this interpretation of the network, our explanation for clusters of behavior provides an endogenously determined explanation for the geographic variation these authors find.31
article.4. Variations on the Basic Model:
In this section we consider the impact of several modeling choices on our results about contagion of ethical and nonethical play. We first ask what happens if nonethical play is not as attractive as in the base model. We then ask about the impact of heterogeneity among the players on how willing they are to play nonethically. We also ask about the implications of assuming that guilt only arises for a player when the other player both expects ethical play and plays ethically.

4.1. Dynamics with nonethical play not dominant (‚Ā†c<a‚Ā†)

In the game described in Section 2.1, withc>a‚Ā†, if players do not experience guilt, then nonethical behavior is a dominant strategy. This makes nonethical behavior a stable strategy in the network regardless of the network structure. We now consider the evolution of play when, perhaps because of regulation or market norms or standards (i.e., the hard and soft infrastructure noted earlier), nonethical behavior is not so powerful. In this section, we analyze the network with the assumptiona>cwhile maintaining the base model parameter assumption.

Given this assumption on parameters, even without guilt there are two pure strategy Nash equilibria in the underlying game: (‚Ā†E,E‚Ā†) and (‚Ā†N,N‚Ā†). We now have a coordination game. The game is complicated, however, by the presence of endogenous guilt, which results in two critical values for cluster density.

Theorem 2 Suppose the parameters of the game satisfy the basic model assumption and a > c ‚Ā† . Consider a network in which all nodes initially play E (or all play N ‚Ā† ). Suppose that a set S of nodes switches to behavior N (or to E ‚Ā† ) and that the process of nodes adjusting their behavior converges. The difference betweenpE‚ąó‚ąóandpN‚ąó‚ąóis determined primarily by the guilt parameterg‚Ā†. Ifg=0‚Ā†, then we have a standard coordination game with a single critical value for cluster density. A related form of history dependence in an overlapping-generations economy is demonstrated inAcemoglu and Jackson (2015). In their model, overlapping generations create a network in which each agent is connected to the one who played before and the one who plays after. These agents pay a cooperation game with two equilibria: one desirable and one not. History dependence occurs because each agent‚Äôs payoff directly depends on the play of the agent who moves before and the one who moves after. As in our analysis, history dependence also arises through its effect on expectations of play. The main differences, other than interpretation, are that we consider a general network and generate history dependence through a psychological game in which guilt depends on expectations about play, which in turn depend on past play.

4.2. Heterogeneous agents

Our agents play ethically if and only if it is in their best interest to do so, as reflected in their payoffs. In our base game, without guilt, nonethical play is a dominant strategy, so we see ethical play only if guilt is strong enough and ethical players are embedded in sufficiently dense clusters. It is also interesting to ask how play evolves if some agents are hardwired to play ethically. These agents play ethically regardless of the play of others. One could model this behavior by attributing different payoffs to these agents, but we find the story more compelling if they are thought of as agents who simply will not play nonethically. In this subsection, we demonstrate how they influence the play of other agents in the network.

Clusters remain critical for understanding the evolution of play in the network. However, in computing the density of a cluster of nodes all playingE‚Ā†, the fraction of ethical playing neighbors for nodes that are hardwired to playEplays no role. So, densities of clusters of nodes playing ethically weakly increase due to the presence of ethical nodes. This can have a significant effect on the spread of nonethical behavior even if there are very few ethical nodes. Then the relevant density for this cluster is one. Nonethical behavior cannot invade.

4.3. Guilt only if the opponent plays ethically

In our base model, a player experiences guilt if he plays nonethically when he expects the other player to expect him to play ethically. This guilt is not dependent on the actual play of the other player, and so guilt is experienced even if the other player actually plays nonethically. An interesting alternative approach is to assume that guilt occurs only if the other player expects ethical play and plays ethically. In this case no one feels guilty playingNif the other player also playsNregardless of expectations. As we will show, this modification makes sustaining ethical play more difficult.

The payoff matrix is now

With our base model assumption (‚Ā†a>c‚ąígandb>g>0‚Ā†), there are now two pure strategy Nash equilibria in the game: (‚Ā†E,E‚Ā†) and (‚Ā†N,N‚Ā†).34The analysis of this game played in a network follows directly from the analysis in Section2. The only difference is that now the density of a cluster of ethical play that is needed to stop a nonethical cascade isq‚ąó‚ąó=ba+b‚ąíc+g>p‚ąó‚ąó‚Ā†. Therefore a node needs a greater fraction of its neighbors to play ethically to induce ethical play.

If individuals experience guilt in an interaction only if the other party to the interaction plays ethically, then clusters of ethical play need to be denser than before to persist. Otherwise, the analysis is unchanged. Thus, sustaining ethical play is now more challenging.
article.5. Expanding the Nature of Guilt: An Ext:
In the base game described in Section2.1, players experience guilt if they disappoint their partner in the game. In this section, we expand the nature of guilt to encompass a broader view, which we capture as the beliefs of ‚Äúsociety.‚ÄĚ This alternative interpretation leads to a similar game in which agents play a coordination game and neither player is directly affected by the beliefs of the other player. Instead, which equilibrium they settle on may impose a negative externality on society, and the players in the game experience guilt if they disappoint society by imposing this externality when it was not expected. This notion of guilt arising from disappointing society seems applicable in many settings (some of which we discuss in the next section), and it has many similarities to Smith‚Äôs ‚Äúimpartial spectator‚ÄĚ discussed earlier whose approbation affects agents‚Äô choices. In our setting, for example, the two players may be negotiating a business deal that can be done either with or without harming society. There is a deal only if the two players coordinate on which way to do the deal; otherwise, nothing happens and payoffs are zero. The potential negative effect on society could be created by a traditional externality (pollution and the like), or it could be a subtle effect such as harming the confidence of society in the workings of the economic system or the fairness of the market.

The players can interact in either of two ways; they can each chose to play strategyEor strategyN‚Ā†. The choice ofEproduces no harmful effects for society, while the choice ofNharms society;Ncreates a negative externality. This setting becomes a psychological game if society is included as a ‚Äúdummy player‚ÄĚ that takes no action but is affected by the actions of the other two players. The two players care about their beliefs about the beliefs held by society and have lower payoffs due to guilt if they disappoint society. Note that if they are expected to choose the externality-producing action, then they do not experience guilt; it is only when they are expected to ‚Äúdo the right thing‚ÄĚ and do not do it that guilt enters. Note also that we are implicitly assuming that society observes the players‚Äô choices in the game. This knowledge is necessary for our interpretation of players‚Äô beliefs about the beliefs of society; society knows what the players did previously and expects them to continue to play as they did, and guilt arises when players who are expected to play ethically play nonethically.

article.6. Building and Sustaining Ethical Marke:
Our analysis demonstrates that ethical outcomes are, at best, fragile. This fragility arises because an individual agent‚Äôs behavior can induce changes in other agents‚Äô behavior, leading to a contagion of nonethical behavior. As discussed earlier, this ‚Äúethical drift‚ÄĚ can have deleterious effects on markets, raising challenges for regulators relying on hard and soft infrastructures to guide market behavior. In this section we consider how to influence outcomes away from nonethical equilibria and toward ethical ones. Our model suggests that a wide range of factors can play a role in affecting market ethics. It is useful to note, however, that the size of agents is not one of these factors. If we view an agent‚Äôs size relative to the entire market as depending on the agent‚Äôs degree and the number of agents in the network, then the number of agents and the number of neighbors any agent interacts with play no role in determining the evolution or the stable configurations of behavior. Instead, what matters from this network point of view for building and sustaining ethical markets are the payoffs in the game and the local structure of the network.

6.1. Game payoffs

As noted in the introduction, agents in our model (at least those not hardwired to play ethically) exhibit a type of ‚Äúrational morality‚ÄĚ in that they play ethically or nonethically depending upon their payoff expectations and network structure. That payoffs play a critical role is not unexpected. In the initial setting considered in Section2, if agents are expected to play nonethically, then the payoff structure makes nonethical behavior a dominant strategy and so cascades of nonethical behavior spread easily through the network. But even when nonethical behavior is not a dominant strategy, the best we can hope for are parameter and network settings in which multiple equilibria can prevail.

How might regulators influence the market toward outcomes with more players adopting ethical behavior? As our model shows, which outcome prevails is determined by the critical density p ‚ąó ‚ąó ‚Ā† , and reducing p ‚ąó ‚ąó makes ethical behavior easier to maintain. It is easy to demonstrate that with our base model assumption, ‚ąā p ‚ąó ‚ąó ‚ąā c > 0 and ‚ąā p ‚ąó ‚ąó ‚ąā b > 0.

So, reducing the payoffs to playing nonethically (thebandc‚Ā†) will result in larger stable clusters of ethical behavior.

Regulation can do so directly via the hard infrastructure by imposing costs to playing nonethically (via increased supervision, fines, or even banishment from the market for more egregious cases), but it can only be successful if it is done on an ex ante, not ex post basis. Punishing people after the fact is standard in financial markets, but it need not deter future bad behavior or move the market to an ethical outcome. Similarly, sporadic enforcement with large consequences in the unlikely event that you get caught may do little to change expectations of how your neighbors will play in the future. Deferred prosecution agreements, probationary periods, or even announcements of target areas of interest to regulators are possible strategies to influence these ex ante payoffs.

In a psychological game setting, the guilt parameter also plays an important role. It is easy to show that‚ąāp‚ąó‚ąó/‚ąāg>0‚Ā†. So, an increase ing‚Ā†, the guilt parameter, will result in larger clusters of ethical behavior. Thus, inducing greater guilt (or aversion to disappointing others) would result in an ethical equilibrium being more likely to prevail.

How might this be accomplished? As we demonstrate in the model, society‚Äôs view on what is considered nonethical can affect equilibrium outcomes. Such societal expectations can be encouraged formally, as captured by the social credit system in Nanjing, China, which categorizes some residents as ‚Äúdiscredited persons‚ÄĚ if they commit too many ‚Äúunsocial actions.‚ÄĚ36The recent joint letter posted by the regulators decrying the ‚Äúopportunistic‚ÄĚ behavior of Blackstone in the CDS market can be similarly effective in changing the market‚Äôs ‚Äúsoft infrastructure‚ÄĚ by resetting expectations of market participants regarding appropriate behavior.37

But it may be even more effective when these societal pressures arise more informally. The Occupy Wall Street movement cast a pall over the financial services industry, inducing at least some to shy away from careers as ‚Äúbanksters‚ÄĚ and spawning a variety of ‚Äúculture‚ÄĚ initiatives for extant bankers.38Another recent example is the decision of the Los Angeles Lakers to return $4.6 million in a Paycheck Protection Program loan when some questioned why a private firm estimated to be worth $4 billion took funds earmarked to help small firms weather the Covid crisis. Because what a player expects others to expect affects the equilibrium, shifting beliefs to expect others to behave ethically can have a substantial effect on the incidence of ethical behavior in the network.

6.2. Network structure

Network structure, and particularly the role of clusters, is fundamental to understanding the spread and incidence of nonethical behavior. Our analysis indicates that network effects arise on both a macro level through the shape of the network and a micro level through the positioning of agents within a network. For the purposes of building and sustaining ethical markets, what matters at either level is the susceptibility of the network to contagion effects.

Our earlier discussion of network structures shows that ethical resilience is not a universal feature. Simple circle-based networks are particularly prone to nonethical behavior spreading fairly easily following any perturbation. This fragility may be one reason why such networks are not generally found in financial markets. More complex star and core-periphery markets, as found, for example, in interbank markets, provide greater robustness, reflecting in part the fact that these market structures can withstand defections of some nodes to nonethical behavior without necessarily causing the entire network to falter. But even in these networks, some nodes have an outsized importance in affecting the spread or curtailment of nonethical behavior.39Viewed from this perspective, actions to sustain ethical markets can be much more effective if they are modeled on the tactics used to stop the spread of a disease or false information rather than on simply punishing miscreants.

Drawing on research on disease dynamics, stopping or preventing a cascade of nonethical behavior in a market requires supporting clusters of ethical behavior. A cluster of ethical nodes can disappear either due to an invasion of nonethical behavior from outside the cluster or because some of the nodes inside the cluster exogenously switch to nonethical behavior. To keep this from happening, regulators need to maintain ‚Äúcritical‚ÄĚ nodes as ethical, but determining this criticality can be complex. Gatekeepers that are hardwired to play ethically are critically important in preventing infection of nonethical behavior from outside of a cluster. It is important for regulators to establish and maintain such nodes. But critical nodes need not be the ones connected to nodes playing nonethically (i.e., ones on the boundary of a cluster ofEs), but can be nodes deep inside of the cluster ofEs. article.7. Conclusion:
We demonstrate a role for endogenously determined, rationally chosen ethics in a financial network setting. We show that the presence of guilt reduces the density of clusters needed to support ethical behavior. This sufficient density, the critical level of density, determines everything: clusters of ethical behavior with at least this density stop the spread of nonethical behavior, and only these sufficiently dense clusters can stop it.

Ethical behavior is generally good for society, or conversely, the externality created by nonethical behavior is bad for society, so it is natural to ask how society can make ethical behavior more stable. Our psychological-games-played-on-a-network model provides answers to this question. We find that ethical behavior is more likely to persist if regulators identify critical nodes, ones whose behavior is critical for the density of ethical clusters or who play the role of gatekeepers, and maintain them as ethical players. We also find that reducing the payoffs to nonethical behavior or increasing the guilt that players experience from unanticipated nonethical play matters if and only if it results in cluster densities moving across the critical level; otherwise, policies that change these payoffs have no effect on the spread of nonethical behavior. These results are (intentionally) reminiscent of the advice that network analysis generates for the control of contagious disease. The details are different (payoffs versus probabilities of infection), but the lesson is the same: interventions need to be big in order to be effective, small interventions are in the long run a waste of effort, and it is important to identify and protect nodes that are critical for the transmission of whatever harmful attribute is spreading through the network.

Our analysis, of course, has several limitations, with perhaps the most important one being the fixed, exogenously given network of homogenous nodes. If some nodes are more likely to play ethically than others, and the network results from nodes‚Äô choices of whom to interact with, then clusters of ethical behavior may arise also because of the endogeneity of the network. More ominous is that nonethical agents may attract other nonethical agents to join the network, turning hitherto ethical markets into unethical venues. We hope to explore these implications of endogenous network formation on market ethics in future research.

Equally important, we believe that the psychological games played on a network approach taken here can be applied more generally to other important research questions. The notion that behaviors are influenced by our expectations of others‚Äô expectations of our behavior is surely applicable in many settings. Moreover, viewing these interactions as occurring in a network can capture how these individual decisions can spread more broadly in equilibrium. Whether it be paying bribes in some developing countries, colluding on some criminal enterprise, or even just opting to choose actions that benefit only you or the entire company speak to the generality of these effects‚ÄĒwhat you expect others to expect of you can have substantial effects on equilibrium outcomes.

33 Our notion of a gatekeeper relates to a node‚Äôs position in the network. This node is special only because of the structure of the network; otherwise it is a typical node. There is a related notion of a gatekeeper in a financial market that appears most prominently the accounting literature; see, for example, Roychowdhury and Srinivasan (2019) . In this literature, a gatekeeper is an agent who has a fiduciary duty to the capital market and who can affect a firm‚Äôs decisions about access to the market.
